metadata.title: Insurance as a Risk Management Tool
metadata.subtitle: Understanding How Insurance Protects Your Financial Future

content.title: Insurance as a Risk Management Tool
content.subtitle: Understanding How Insurance Protects Your Financial Future

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Sarah, a 28-year-old graphic designer, needs an appendectomy costing $20,000 ..

How do insurance components like deductibles and co-insurance work together to determine your actual out-of-pocket costs?

slides.speakerNotes: Read Sarah's appendectomy scenario aloud. Ask: "Before we calculate, guess how much Sarah will pay out of $20,000." Record guesses to compare after the lesson.

objectives.description: Understand key insurance concepts: premiums, deductibles, co-insurance, coverage limits, and exclusions.
Identify different types of insurance (health, auto, home, life) and their specific purposes.
Calculate actual out-of-pocket costs when insurance claims are filed.
Apply insurance knowledge to make informed decisions about coverage needs for different life situations.

terms.definition: The amount paid regularly (monthly, quarterly, annually) to maintain insurance coverage, regardless of whether claims are filed.
terms.example: Sarah pays $300/month for health insurance, totaling $3,600/year whether she uses it or not.

terms.definition: The amount you must pay out-of-pocket before your insurance coverage begins to pay.
terms.example: With a $2,000 deductible, Sarah pays the first $2,000 of her appendectomy costs herself.

terms.definition: A percentage of costs you share with the insurance company after meeting your deductible. An 80/20 plan means insurance pays 80%, you pay 20%.
terms.example: After Sarah's $2,000 deductible, she pays 20% of the remaining $18,000 = $3,600.

terms.definition: The maximum amount an insurance policy will pay for a covered loss. Costs above this limit are your responsibility.
terms.example: Auto liability of $100,000/$300,000 means $100K max per person, $300K max per accident.

leftPanel.title: Risk Transfer in Action
paragraphs: Insurance transfers financial risk from individuals to insurance companies.
You pay regular premiums in exchange for protection against potentially devastating losses.
The insurance company pools money from many policyholders to pay claims for the few who suffer losses.

highlightBox.text: Insurance works best for high-impact, low-probability events—rare but costly occurrences.

infoCard.title: Cost Sharing Layers
items: 2. Deductible: Your first payment at claim

layoutData.title: Different Policies for Different Risks
paragraphs: Each type of insurance serves a specific purpose in your overall financial protection:
bulletPoints: Health Insurance: Covers medical expenses—doctor visits, hospital stays, prescriptions, surgeries
Auto Insurance: Liability (injuries/damage you cause), collision (damage to your car), comprehensive (theft, weather)
Homeowner's/Renter's: Property damage + personal liability + theft protection
Life Insurance: Provides financial support to dependents after your death
Disability Insurance: Replaces income if you're unable to work due to illness or injury

keyPoint.text: ⚠️ Common exclusion: Most homeowner's policies do NOT cover flood damage—separate flood insurance is required.

layoutData.title: How Sarah's $20,000 Surgery Gets Paid
paragraphs: Let's calculate Sarah's actual out-of-pocket cost for her appendectomy:

keyPoint.text: 📊 Without insurance, Sarah would pay $20,000. With insurance, she pays $5,600—a savings of $14,400.

leftColumn.title: Coverage for Others
items: Required by law in most states

rightColumn.title: Coverage for Your Car
items: Value based on car's actual cash value
May not be worth it on older cars

content.headerTitle: Life Insurance: Term vs. Whole Life

leftColumn.title: Term Life Insurance
items: Coverage for a specific period (10, 20, 30 years)
Expires if you outlive the term

rightColumn.title: Whole Life Insurance
items: Never expires as long as premiums paid

leftPanel.title: Limits Protect the Insurance Company—Not Just You
paragraphs: Coverage limits cap what the insurance company will pay. Costs above the limit are YOUR responsibility.
Example: Marcus has auto liability limits of $100,000/$300,000.
He causes an accident injuring two people:
• Person A has $120,000 in medical bills
• Person B has $80,000 in medical bills
Insurance pays: Person A gets $100,000 (the per-person limit), Person B gets $80,000.
Marcus owes Person A the remaining $20,000 out of pocket.

slides.speakerNotes:
COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS:
• ""After I pay the deductible, insurance covers everything."" → After the deductible, you still pay co-insurance (your percentage) until you reach the out-of-pocket maximum.
• ""Homeowner's insurance covers flood damage."" → Standard homeowner's policies exclude floods. You need a separate flood insurance policy, often through FEMA's National Flood Insurance Program.
• ""Young people don't need life insurance."" → Life insurance is actually cheapest when you're young and healthy. If you have dependents or co-signed debt, you may need coverage.

layoutData.title: What Insurance Typically Doesn't Cover
paragraphs: Every insurance policy has exclusions—situations or damages NOT covered. Know them BEFORE you need to file a claim:
bulletPoints: Homeowner's: Flood damage, earthquake damage, normal wear and tear, intentional damage
Health: Cosmetic procedures, experimental treatments, pre-existing conditions (varies by plan)
Auto: Intentional damage, racing, using car for ride-share without proper coverage
Life: Suicide within first 2 years, death during illegal activities, fraud on application

keyPoint.text: 📋 In {{STATE_NAME}}, flood insurance is purchased separately through the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP).

scenario.name: Aisha's Apartment Fire
paragraphs: A fire damages Aisha's apartment, destroying $15,000 worth of personal belongings.
Her renter's insurance has a $500 deductible and a $20,000 coverage limit.

scenario.name: New Parent's Life Insurance Decision
paragraphs: Marcus, age 30, has a newborn child. His spouse is a stay-at-home parent.
He's choosing between term life ($30/month for $500,000 coverage for 20 years) or whole life ($250/month for $500,000 permanent coverage).

content.question:
Jordan, age 22, is choosing between employer health insurance ($200/month premium, $1,500 deductible) or staying on parents' plan (free) for 4 more years.

What factors should Jordan consider?

What situations might make the employer plan worth the cost?

Think about your family's current insurance coverage.

Which type of insurance do you think is most important for your family's situation, and why?

Is there any coverage gap that concerns you?

questions.question: Which common type of damage is typically NOT covered by homeowner's insurance?
Explain the difference between term life and whole life insurance. When might each be appropriate?
Auto liability limits are $100,000/$300,000. You cause an accident injuring two people: $120,000 and $80,000. What does insurance pay each?
A health plan has a $1,500 deductible and 80/20 co-insurance. For a $10,000 bill, calculate total patient out-of-pocket cost.

slides.speakerNotes:
ASSESSMENT ANSWER KEY:
Q5 (Jordan):Consider: coverage quality, network of doctors, future health needs, cost if staying free, independence/portability, tax benefits of own plan. There's no single right answer.
CYU 1:C (Flood damage) — Floods require separate flood insurance policy.
CYU 2:Term life: Temporary coverage for a set period (10-30 years), lower premiums, no cash value. Good for young families needing coverage while children grow up. Whole life: Permanent coverage for entire life, higher premiums, builds cash value. Good for estate planning or lifelong dependents.
CYU 3:Person A: $100,000 (capped at per-person limit). Person B: $80,000 (full amount, under limit). Total: $180,000, which is under the $300,000 per-accident limit. Marcus pays $20,000 out of pocket for Person A's remaining bills.
CYU 4:$15,000 - $500 deductible =$14,500(well under the $20,000 coverage limit).

takeaways.title: Four Cost Components
takeaways.description: Premium (regular payment), deductible (first pay), co-insurance (shared %), coverage limit (max payout).

takeaways.title: Match Insurance to Need
takeaways.description: Health, auto, home, life, and disability insurance each serve specific financial protection purposes.

takeaways.title: Know Your Exclusions
takeaways.description: Every policy has things it doesn't cover—read the fine print before you need to file a claim.

takeaways.title: Calculate True Costs
takeaways.description: Out-of-pocket = deductible + co-insurance (up to out-of-pocket max). Do the math before choosing a plan.

main.title: Insurance Policy Comparison Lab
main.description: You've learned how insurance works—but can you compare real policies? Tomorrow you'll analyze actual insurance quotes and calculate total costs for different life scenarios.

steps: Compare three health insurance plans for a hypothetical scenario
Calculate total annual costs (premiums + likely out-of-pocket)
Analyze auto insurance quotes for different coverage levels
Determine appropriate life insurance amounts based on family needs
Create a personalized insurance recommendations report
